explanation: We interpret 'If prop_0, explain how prop_1' as 'prop_0 implies prop_1', i.e., whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 also holds at the same moment. In LTL, this is captured by the globally operator applied to the implication: globally, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.

The natural language phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as a simple implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is represented as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

The statement means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should also hold. This is a classic implication. In LTL, we enforce that globally, if prop_0 is true at any point, then prop_1 must also be true at that point.

This sentence requires that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that moment (so that the explanation is present exactly when prop_0 holds). In LTL, this is usually captured as 'globally, if prop_0 then prop_1'.

The phrase 'If prop_0, explain how prop_1' is ambiguous but assuming a temporal logic translation, it can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, eventually prop_1 must hold. In other words, at every point where prop_0 is true, prop_1 must happen at some point in the future. In LTL, this is captured as G(prop_0 -> F prop_1).

This statement can be interpreted as an implication: whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true as a kind of 'explanation.' In LTL, using 'G' for 'always', we express that globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds.

The statement 'If prop_0, explain how prop_1' can be interpreted as: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 should also hold at the same time. This is a standard implication in temporal logic, expressed globally.

The formula 'G(prop_0 -> F prop_1)' means that whenever 'prop_0' holds, then eventually 'prop_1' must hold at some point in the future. This captures the intuition that if 'prop_0' occurs, it should be followed at some later point by 'prop_1'.

This statement points to a co-occurrence without any temporal relation, which is generally represented as 'if prop_0 then prop_1'. In LTL, this is globally: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is expressed as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)'.

The natural language statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' corresponds to the implication 'if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true' at all times. In LTL, this is represented as 'Globally, prop_0 implies prop_1'.
